core_intuitions: Soft prompting makes the prompt EMBEDDINGS trainable while the backbone stays frozen; gradients reach only the k soft tokens (already served by the existing figure).

The three input/activation-space PEFT methods differ by WHERE they intervene: prompt tuning at the input layer only, prefix tuning by prepending learned K/V at EVERY attention layer, IA3 by element-wise rescaling of K, V, and FFN activations at every layer.
A task vector is just the delta between a fine-tune and its base (theta_ft - theta_base); skills can be ADDED, SUBTRACTED, and COMPOSED by arithmetic on these vectors in weight space.

Merging only works because fine-tunes from the SAME base checkpoint live in a shared low-loss basin, so their interpolation stays low-loss for multiple tasks; different-base models live in unrelated basins and merging is noise.

Naive summation of task vectors causes INTERFERENCE (tasks push the same parameter in opposite directions); TIES resolves it by trimming small entries, electing a majority sign per position, and averaging only the agreeing values.

existing.name: softprompt-frozen-backbone-gradient-flow
existing.issue: Clearly conveys the core soft-prompt idea (trainable embeddings, frozen backbone, gradient flowing only to soft tokens) and reads correctly in both themes. Minor: in dark mode the 'frozen' dark boxes (input tokens, inner FFN panel) sit dark-on-dark with only a faint border, so contrast is a touch weak, but all labels remain legible and the trainable/frozen distinction is unambiguous.
existing.improvement: Optional only: bump the border/stroke contrast on the 'frozen' boxes in dark mode so they separate more crisply from the page background. No structural change needed.

new_figures.name: peft-intervention-sites
new_figures.concept: A single transformer cross-section showing where prompt tuning, prefix tuning, and IA3 each attach.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's first half introduces three methods whose whole distinction is the LOCATION of intervention in the network. Prose plus a params-table cannot convey the spatial mental model that makes them click: input-layer-only vs per-layer-KV vs per-layer-elementwise-scale. One unified cut-away diagram lets a reader hold all three in one picture and see instantly why prefix/IA3 give a per-layer signal while prompt tuning only touches layer 0.
new_figures.placement: Soft Prompts: Motivation and Taxonomy (immediately after the three-family comparison table, before the Prompt Tuning section)
new_figures.brief: Draw a vertical transformer stack as the spine: an 'input embeddings' row at the bottom, then 3 stacked identical transformer layers (label the middle stack region 'x L layers', show each layer as a block containing a small 'self-attention' sub-box with Q, K, V ports and an 'FFN' sub-box), then 'output logits' at the top. Reading order bottom to top. Overlay three color-neutral, clearly-distinguished intervention callouts, each with its own legend swatch and a short label:  (1) PROMPT TUNING - a small bracket of k little 'soft token' cells attached ONLY at the input-embeddings row, feeding into layer 1; annotate 'k learned embeddings, input layer only'. (2) PREFIX TUNING - at EVERY layer's attention sub-box, show a small stack of k learned K/V cells being prepended to that layer's K and V ports (a little [P_K; K] / [P_V; V] concat glyph); annotate 'learned K/V prepended at every layer'. Use a repeated marker on each of the L layers (e.g. the same small tag on layer 1, the middle 'x L' block, and layer L) so the reader sees it is per-layer. (3) IA3 - at every layer show three small circular multiply (odot) glyphs: one on the K path, one on the V path, one on the FFN intermediate activation, each labeled with its scale vector l_k, l_v, l_ff; annotate 'element-wise scale on K, V, FFN'. Make clear the backbone weights are frozen (a small lock on the layer blocks) and each method adds a DIFFERENT small trainable object. Keep the three intervention styles visually separable by shape/pattern as well as by legend, not color alone, so it survives both themes. Fit everything inside the viewBox; do not let callouts overlap the spine labels. No invented benchmark numbers; k and L are the only integers, kept symbolic.

new_figures.name: task-vector-arithmetic-basin
new_figures.concept: Task vectors as arrows from a shared base checkpoint, composed by arithmetic, all living inside one low-loss basin.
new_figures.rationale: This is the single load-bearing intuition of the entire merging half of the chapter: (a) tau = theta_ft - theta_base is a direction in weight space, (b) add/subtract/compose is literal vector arithmetic, and (c) it only works because same-base fine-tunes share a convex low-loss basin. A static geometric picture makes all three land at once and directly grounds the later 'merging fails across different base checkpoints' warning.
new_figures.placement: Task Arithmetic (right after the task-vector definition and the add/remove/combine bullet list)
new_figures.brief: Draw a 2D weight-space schematic (label axes abstractly as 'weight-space, 2 of millions of dims'). Show a shaded rounded 'low-loss basin' region (like a soft contour bowl) with a labeled point theta_base near its center. From theta_base draw two solid arrows to two fine-tuned points: theta_A ('task A fine-tune') and theta_B ('task B fine-tune'); label each arrow as the task vector tau_A and tau_B. Then illustrate composition: from theta_base draw a third, visually distinct arrow equal to tau_A + tau_B landing on a 'merged' point theta_merge = theta_base + tau_A + tau_B, and show it still sits INSIDE the basin (this is the whole point). Include a small inset or side note showing the SUBTRACTION case: theta_base - tau_A as a dashed arrow pointing the opposite direction, labeled 'negate a behavior'. Then, clearly separated (a divider or a faded second panel to the side), show a SECOND basin far away with a lone point theta_other labeled 'different base checkpoint - unrelated basin', with a crossed-out dashed arrow between the two basins labeled 'merging across bases = noise'. Keep arrows non-overlapping and clearly directed; label the parallelogram-style vector addition so the arithmetic reads as literal vector sum. Theme-safe: use neutral fills plus stroke/pattern distinctions, no hardcoded primary colors; the only 'numbers' are symbolic (tau_A, tau_B, lambda not required). Everything inside the viewBox, generous label spacing.

new_figures.name: ties-merge-pipeline
new_figures.concept: TIES three-stage pipeline (Trim, Elect Sign, Disjoint Merge) operating on a small grid of parameters from three task vectors.
new_figures.rationale: TIES is the most mechanically intricate algorithm in the chapter and its value (killing sign interference) is invisible in prose. Walking a tiny concrete grid of parameters through trim -> elect-sign -> disjoint-average makes the interference problem and its fix tangible: the reader SEES a position where two tasks disagree in sign and watches the minority get dropped before averaging.
new_figures.placement: TIES: Trim, Elect Sign, Disjoint Merge (after the three-step equations, before the code block)
new_figures.brief: Three side-by-side stages, left to right, arrows between them, operating on the SAME small illustrative example: 3 task vectors (tau_1, tau_2, tau_3), each a short row of ~6 parameter cells shown as signed magnitude bars (some tall +, some tall -, some tiny near-zero). Use small illustrative integers/signs only (e.g. +3, -1, 0, +2), no benchmark numbers. STAGE 1 TRIM: show each row with its small-magnitude cells greyed/zeroed out (keep only top-magnitude entries), annotate 'keep top-p% by |magnitude|, zero the rest - removes conflict-prone noise'. STAGE 2 ELECT SIGN: pick ONE highlighted parameter column where the three trimmed vectors disagree (e.g. +, +, -). Show a per-column sign vote: sum the signs, the majority sign wins; draw the elected sign row gamma with a clear '+ wins here (2 vs 1)' callout. This is the crux column - make it the visual hero. STAGE 3 DISJOINT MERGE: for the hero column, show the minority (the '-') entry crossed out/excluded, and the merged value = average of ONLY the agreeing entries; annotate 'average only the entries that match the elected sign'. End with a single merged task-vector row tau_merge, and a small tag 'theta_merge = theta_base + lambda * tau_merge'. Contrast this against a faint 'naive sum' ghost showing the conflicting column would have partially cancelled - so the reader sees what interference TIES avoided. Keep the grid legible, cells aligned in columns across all three stages so the eye can track one parameter through the pipeline. Theme-safe: encode sign by shape/fill-direction and label, not by red/green alone; all inside viewBox.

summary: The chapter is concept-rich but figure-poor: one strong existing figure (soft-prompt gradient flow, verdict KEEP) covers only the first of five core intuitions. I propose three new STATIC figures, each targeting a foundational mechanism prose cannot convey spatially: (1) peft-intervention-sites - a unified transformer cross-section showing WHERE prompt/prefix/IA3 each attach, the whole point of the taxonomy; (2) task-vector-arithmetic-basin - task vectors as arrows from a shared base plus the shared-low-loss-basin intuition that is the load-bearing idea of the merging half and grounds the 'different base = noise' warning; (3) ties-merge-pipeline - a tiny concrete grid walked through trim/elect-sign/disjoint-merge so sign interference and its fix become tangible. All static because a single frame suffices; no animation is essential here. Priorities 1,1,2.
